content:
# Exodus Chapter 38 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), pp. 109-110; Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 81-82 | **Source Images:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_038_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_038_page_002.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_038_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_038_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## The Bronze Altar (vv. 1-7)

**1** And he made the altar of the burnt offering of acacia wood; five cubits its length and five cubits its width -- square -- and three cubits its height.

**2** And he made its horns on its four corners -- from it were its horns -- and he overlaid it with bronze.

**3** And he made all the vessels of the altar: the pots and the shovels and the basins and the forks and the fire-pans; all its vessels he made of bronze.

**4** And he made for the altar a grating, a work of network of bronze, under its ledge, from beneath, unto its middle.

**5** And he cast four rings on the four ends of the grating of bronze -- holders for the poles.

**6** And he made the poles of acacia wood, and he overlaid them with bronze.

**7** And he brought the poles into the rings on the sides of the altar, to carry it with them; hollow of boards he made it.

---

## The Bronze Basin (v. 8)

**8** And he made the basin of bronze and its stand of bronze, with the mirrors of the women who served, who served at the entrance of the tent of meeting.

---

## The Courtyard (vv. 9-20)

**9** And he made the courtyard: for the south side southward, the hangings of the courtyard of twisted linen, a hundred cubits.

**10** Their pillars -- twenty, and their bases -- twenty, of bronze; the hooks of the pillars and their bands -- silver.

**11** And for the north side, a hundred cubits; their pillars -- twenty, and their bases -- twenty, of bronze; the hooks of the pillars and their bands -- silver.

**12** And for the west side, hangings -- fifty cubits; their pillars -- ten, and their bases -- ten; the hooks of the pillars and their bands -- silver.

**13** And for the east side eastward -- fifty cubits.

**14** Hangings -- fifteen cubits -- to the one shoulder; their pillars -- three, and their bases -- three.

**15** And for the second shoulder -- on this side and on that side of the gate of the courtyard -- hangings -- fifteen cubits; their pillars -- three, and their bases -- three.

**16** All the hangings of the courtyard all around -- twisted linen.

**17** And the bases for the pillars -- bronze; the hooks of the pillars and their bands -- silver; and the overlaying of their tops -- silver; and they were banded with silver -- all the pillars of the courtyard.

**18** And the screen of the gate of the courtyard -- the work of an embroiderer -- of blue and purple and scarlet yarn and twisted linen; and twenty cubits the length, and the height in the width -- five cubits, corresponding to the hangings of the courtyard.

**19** And their pillars -- four, and their bases -- four -- bronze; their hooks -- silver, and the overlaying of their tops and their bands -- silver.

**20** And all the pegs for the tabernacle and for the courtyard all around -- bronze.

---

## The Accounting of the Tabernacle (vv. 21-31)

**21** These are the reckonings of the tabernacle -- the tabernacle of the testimony -- which were reckoned at the command of Moses, the service of the Levites by the hand of Ithamar, son of Aaron the priest.

**22** And Bezalel son of Uri, son of Hur, of the tribe of Judah, made all that YHWH commanded Moses.

**23** And with him -- Oholiab son of Ahisamach, of the tribe of Dan -- an engraver and a designer and an embroiderer in the blue and in the purple and in the scarlet yarn and in the linen.

**24** All the gold that was used for the work, in all the work of the holy place -- and the gold of the wave offering was twenty-nine talents and seven hundred and thirty shekels, by the shekel of the holy place.

**25** And the silver of the numbered ones of the congregation -- a hundred talents and a thousand and seven hundred and seventy-five shekels, by the shekel of the holy place.

**26** A beka per head -- a half of the shekel, by the shekel of the holy place -- for everyone who passed over to the numbered ones, from twenty years old and upward, for six hundred thousand and three thousand and five hundred and fifty.

**27** And there were the hundred talents of silver to cast the bases of the holy place and the bases of the veil -- a hundred bases for the hundred talents, a talent per base.

**28** And from the thousand and seven hundred and seventy-five he made hooks for the pillars, and he overlaid their tops and banded them.

**29** And the bronze of the wave offering -- seventy talents and two thousand and four hundred shekels.

**30** And he made with it the bases of the entrance of the tent of meeting, and the bronze altar and the bronze grating that was for it, and all the vessels of the altar,

**31** and the bases of the courtyard all around, and the bases of the gate of the courtyard, and all the pegs of the tabernacle, and all the pegs of the courtyard all around.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

### Manuscript Observations

The Leningrad Codex (B19A) presents Exodus 38 across pages 109-110 in the standard three-column format with full Tiberian vocalization and cantillation. The chapter divides into two structurally distinct sections: the construction of the bronze altar, basin, and courtyard enclosure (vv. 1-20), and the material accounting of the tabernacle (vv. 21-31). A parashah setumah break is visible between verse 20 and verse 21, marking this major structural division. The shift is signaled by the phrase elleh fequdei ha-mishkan ("these are the reckonings of the tabernacle"), introducing the only passage in the entire construction narrative that quantifies materials and links silver to the census half-shekel. The density of numerical terms in verses 24-31 is notable, with compound numbers precisely vocalized throughout. Plene and defective spellings are consistent with scribal practice across the codex.

The Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209, pp. 81-82) presents the material corresponding to MT Exodus 38 in broadly parallel form -- a significant contrast with the dramatic divergence seen in MT Exodus 37, where the furniture-construction narrative was largely absent from the LXX. The Vaticanus pages, headed CAP. XXVIII, show three-column uncial scriptio continua containing references to the thusiasterion (altar), the loutera/loutera (basin), and the aulen (courtyard). The bronze altar, courtyard construction, and material accounting sections all appear in recognizable form. The most notable textual divergence is in verse 8: where the MT reads bemar'ot hatsove'ot ("with the mirrors of the women who served"), the LXX tradition preserves a variant reading ton nesteusason ("of the fasting women"), interpreting the women's activity as ascetic practice rather than organized service. This represents a significant interpretive divergence, likely reflecting a different vocalization tradition or theological emphasis in the Greek translation. The accounting section shows some variation in ordering and the interleaving of priestly-garment material (corresponding to MT 39) within the LXX chapter 38, but the numerical figures for gold, silver, and bronze largely agree with the MT.

### Theological Architecture of the Chapter

Exodus 38 completes the fulfillment narrative for every object instructed in Exodus 25-30. The bronze altar (vv. 1-7) parallels Exodus 27:1-8; the basin (v. 8) parallels 30:17-21; the courtyard (vv. 9-20) parallels 27:9-19. With this chapter, the construction of every commanded item is recorded. The final section (vv. 21-31) has no parallel in the instruction text -- it is new material unique to the fulfillment narrative, adding an administrative and quantitative dimension that YHWH did not command. God commanded the building; Moses commanded the audit. This distinction between divine blueprint and human accountability is the chapter's distinctive theological contribution.

**The Two-Part Structure (Construction and Accounting):** The chapter moves from building to counting, from craft to census. Verses 1-20 conclude the spatial-material construction (moving outward from the interior furnishings of chapter 37 to the bronze altar, basin, and courtyard perimeter). Verses 21-31 then halt the narrative to take inventory. Before the tabernacle can be erected and filled with divine glory (chapter 40), every contribution must be acknowledged, every resource traced to its source, every person counted. The accounting is itself an act of worship -- a demonstration that the people's offerings were used exactly as intended, with no material lost to private hands.

**The Bronze Basin from Mirrors (v. 8):** The basin made from women's mirrors is the only piece of tabernacle furniture whose raw material is traced to a specific group of donors and whose prior function is named. The transformation is theologically potent: polished bronze mirrors -- instruments of self-contemplation -- are reshaped into the basin of priestly purification, the vessel through which Aaron and his sons wash their hands and feet before approaching the altar or entering the tent (30:18-21). The women who donated these mirrors "served" (tsave'u, from tsava, "to serve/assemble in organized duty") at the tent entrance, a term suggesting recognized service at the sanctuary -- the same root used for Levitical duty in Numbers 4:23 and 8:24. The LXX tradition's rendering "fasting women" (nesteusason) reflects a different interpretive tradition, but the MT's "serving women" preserves the connection to organized sanctuary ministry.

**The Census Silver and the Theology of Equal Foundation (vv. 25-28):** The silver came not from voluntary offering but from the mandatory census tax: one beka (half-shekel) per head from every man twenty years old and upward. The total -- 603,550 men -- matches Numbers 1:46 precisely, and the mathematics are internally exact: 603,550 half-shekels = 301,775 shekels; at 3,000 shekels per talent, that yields 100 talents and 1,775 shekels. The 100 talents became the 100 bases (adanim) on which the entire tabernacle rests -- one talent per base. The sanctuary's foundation is thus composed of the equal contribution of every Israelite man. The rich gave no more, the poor no less (30:15). Every member of the covenant community is present in the foundation of God's dwelling, and no individual's contribution is identifiable in the finished base. The remaining 1,775 shekels furnished the pillar hooks, capitals, and connecting bands -- every fraction accounted for.

**The Three Metals and the Gradient of Holiness (vv. 24-31):** The accounting quantifies the Tabernacle's metal hierarchy: 29 talents of gold (innermost, most holy objects), 100 talents of silver (structural foundations and connectors), 70 talents of bronze (outer courtyard infrastructure and pegs). Gold predominates near the divine presence (ark, atonement-cover, lampstand, incense altar). Silver forms the structural foundation -- the bases into which the tabernacle frames are inserted. Bronze constitutes the outermost and most utilitarian elements: the altar of sacrifice, the courtyard bases, the tent pegs driven into the ground. The gradient -- gold, silver, bronze -- corresponds to proximity to YHWH: the closer to the divine throne, the more precious the metal. Silver exceeds the others in quantity because it bears the most extensive structural burden, and because it derives from the entire people's contribution rather than from voluntary gifts alone.

### Key Consensus Decisions

1. **"Reckonings" for fequdei (v. 21):** EMET renders "reckonings," LOGOS renders "accounts," PNEUMA renders "inventory." SOPHIA adopts "reckonings" (EMET) because it preserves the root p-q-d ("to visit, number, reckon") and maintains the connection to the parashah name Pekudei. "Accounts" (LOGOS) is acceptable but modern-financial in connotation. "Inventory" (PNEUMA) is a purely commercial term that loses the theological freight of fequdei -- this is not mere bookkeeping but a solemn mustering and reckoning before YHWH.

2. **"Laver/basin" for kiyyor (v. 8):** EMET renders "laver," LOGOS renders "basin," PNEUMA renders "basin." SOPHIA adopts "basin" (LOGOS/PNEUMA majority) because "laver" is archaic and unfamiliar to most modern English readers, while "basin" accurately conveys the vessel's function for washing. The Hebrew kiyyor designates a container for water, and "basin" is the most transparent English equivalent.

3. **"Women who served" for hatsove'ot (v. 8):** All three translators render the Hebrew tsove'ot as "women who served" (EMET/LOGOS) or "women who served" (PNEUMA). SOPHIA concurs. The root tsava carries the sense of organized duty or mustered service, matching its use for Levitical service in Numbers 4:23 and 8:24. The LXX variant "fasting women" (nesteusason) is noted but not adopted; SOPHIA follows the MT consonantal text and its Tiberian vocalization.

4. **"Veil" for parokhet (v. 27):** EMET renders "curtain," LOGOS renders "veil," PNEUMA renders "curtain." SOPHIA adopts "veil" (LOGOS) because parokhet designates the inner screen separating the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place, a dividing barrier that conceals. "Curtain" (EMET/PNEUMA) is generic and does not distinguish the parokhet from the masakh (entrance screen). English "veil" preserves the concealing function and the theological significance of the barrier -- the same term used in the New Testament for what was torn at the crucifixion (Mark 15:38, citing this very parokhet).

5. **"Beka" for beqa (v. 26):** EMET renders "bekah," LOGOS renders "beka," PNEUMA renders "beka." SOPHIA adopts "beka" (LOGOS/PNEUMA majority) as the transliteration of beqa, a technical term for the half-shekel weight. EMET's "bekah" adds a final 'h' not present in the Hebrew consonantal form. The term is sufficiently technical that transliteration is appropriate; the verse itself defines it: "a half of the shekel."

6. **"The numbered ones" for ha-pequdim (v. 26):** EMET renders "the numbered-ones," LOGOS renders "those who were numbered," PNEUMA renders "everyone who was counted in the census." SOPHIA adopts "the numbered ones" (EMET, without hyphens) because it preserves the participial form of the Hebrew and maintains the p-q-d root connection to fequdei ("reckonings") in verse 21. PNEUMA's "everyone who was counted in the census" is a full paraphrase that dilutes the verbal echo between verses 21 and 26.

7. **"Ledge" for karkubb (v. 4):** EMET renders "ledge," LOGOS renders "ledge," PNEUMA omits the term (paraphrasing as "the altar's ledge"). SOPHIA adopts "ledge" (EMET/LOGOS) because karkubb designates the projecting rim or shelf around the altar's midsection, and "ledge" accurately captures this architectural feature.

8. **"Holders" for batim (v. 5):** EMET renders "holders," LOGOS renders "holders," PNEUMA renders "to hold the carrying poles." SOPHIA adopts "holders" (EMET/LOGOS) because the Hebrew batim (literally "houses") designates receptacles or housings for the poles. PNEUMA converts the noun into a purpose clause, losing the concrete image of the "houses" that receive the poles.

9. **"Twisted linen" for shesh moshzar (vv. 9, 16, 18):** EMET renders "twisted linen," LOGOS renders "fine twisted linen," PNEUMA renders "finely woven linen." SOPHIA adopts "twisted linen" (EMET) because the Hebrew shesh means "linen" (not "fine linen" -- that would require the modifier bad or a separate adjective), and moshzar means "twisted/braided." LOGOS adds "fine" as an interpretive supplement; PNEUMA replaces "twisted" with "woven," which describes a different textile process. The LXX's byssou keklosmenes ("of spun linen") supports "twisted" as the process.

10. **"Bands" for khashukeihem (vv. 10-12, 17, 19):** EMET renders "bands," LOGOS renders "bands," PNEUMA renders "connecting rods." SOPHIA adopts "bands" (EMET/LOGOS) because the Hebrew khashukeihem (from khashaq, "to bind, to fillet") designates the silver connectors binding the courtyard pillars together. "Connecting rods" (PNEUMA) introduces a mechanical image not warranted by the Hebrew root, which suggests wrapping or binding rather than rigid rod construction.

11. **"Shoulder" for katef (vv. 14-15):** EMET renders "shoulder," LOGOS renders "side," PNEUMA renders "side of the entrance." SOPHIA adopts "shoulder" (EMET) because katef literally means "shoulder" -- the flanking curtain sections beside the gate opening, metaphorically likened to a body's shoulders on either side of the head (the gate). The architectural metaphor is deliberate and should be preserved. "Side" (LOGOS) is generic and loses the embodied imagery.

12. **"Banded" for mekhushshaqim (v. 17):** EMET renders "banded," LOGOS renders "banded," PNEUMA omits the specific term (saying "banded with silver"). SOPHIA adopts "banded" (EMET/LOGOS) -- the Pual participle of khashaq, describing every courtyard pillar as wrapped with silver fillets. All three translators agree on the sense; the only variation is PNEUMA's paraphrastic integration.

13. **"He overlaid their tops and banded them" (v. 28):** EMET renders "he overlaid their tops and he banded them," LOGOS renders "he overlaid their tops and banded them," PNEUMA renders "overlaid their tops, and made their connecting rods." SOPHIA adopts "he overlaid their tops and banded them" (LOGOS), preserving both verbs (tsippah, "overlaid"; khishshaq, "banded") without PNEUMA's substitution of "connecting rods" for the banding action.

14. **"Work of an embroiderer" for ma'aseh roqem (v. 18):** EMET renders "work of an embroiderer," LOGOS renders "work of an embroiderer," PNEUMA renders "work of a skilled embroiderer." SOPHIA adopts "work of an embroiderer" (EMET/LOGOS) because the Hebrew roqem means "embroiderer" without an added intensifier. PNEUMA's "skilled" is not present in the Hebrew, following the same principle applied to "master perfumer" in 37:29.

15. **"Overlaying of their tops" for tsippui rasheihem (vv. 17, 19):** EMET renders "overlaying of their tops," LOGOS renders "overlaying of their tops," PNEUMA renders "silver-overlaid tops." SOPHIA adopts "overlaying of their tops" (EMET/LOGOS) because it preserves the construct chain as a nominal phrase -- the text names the overlaying as a distinct element in the pillar description, not merely an adjectival modifier of the tops.
